details.text: - صَدِيقٌ حَمِيمٌ is a close or intimate friend.
Derivative: الأَرْذَلُونَ – Those who are less or lower than anything or everything else. The undesirables and unliked within the society.

Derivative: طَارِدِ means to push away due to a belief that they are inferior, worthless or despicable. طَرَدَ means when you distance, drive or push someone away because you believe them to be contemptable or depraved.

- فَافْتَحْ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحًا can mean: “Make a decision or pass a judgment between me and them which is absolutely final” or “Make an opening between me and them which is clear whereby we are completely separated and no longer have anything to do with each other”.
Derivative: مَشْحُون means fully loaded. It is from شَحَنَ which means to load and fill up.

Derivative: يُكَذِّبُونِ from كَذَّبَ – تَكْذِيب and it means to deny something or someone or to call something a lie or someone a liar. Here, in this verse it means that they will deny me or the message, according to the first meaning, or they will call my message a lie or me a liar, according to the second meaning.
Derivative: تَعْبَثُونَ from عَبِثَ which means to do something without purpose, worth or practical benefit.
Derivative: Sign, symbol and monument. When it relates to building then it refers to a monument i.e., something visual for passersby to see.
Derivative: An elevated place.

Derivative: يَضِيقُ is from ضِيق the opposite of سَعَة (expanse). It means tightness or be constricted. It can mean poverty (or bankruptcy) and being in a bad situation. سَعَة (expanse), its opposite, is also used for wealth to mean affluence. For the chest to suffer from ضِيق indicates to being afflicted by a great stress and distress, like the feel one can’t breathe because his chest is so tight.

Derivative: يَنطَلِقُ from اِنْطَلَقَ which means to set off, go ahead, to march forward and move into action. Here it is in reference to the tongue not being able to move forward and into action as it should i.e., the inability to speak.
Derivative: أَهْلَكْنَا from أَهْلَكَ literally means to provide a violent death.

Derivative: ذَنْبٌ means a sin that you are embarrassed about. ذَنْبٌ in itself may not be an evil deed unlike سَيِّئَة which is an evil deed. In respect to ذَنْبٌ, the Arabs state that its consequences are no good. An act may be justifiable but the outcome of it made it a ذَنْبٌ. In this verse it means that they have something against me, that I did and the outcome of it was really bad i.e., referring to Musa striking and killing a man.
Derivative: فَارِهِينَ means to be proficient and skilful. It can also imply showing off.
Derivative: كَلَّا means: “On the contrary” or “certainly not” and is said in response to someone saying something will happen to negate its occurrence with emphasis and certainty.
Derivative: المُسَحَّرِينَ means to be bewitched or enchanted repeatedly until the enchantment has become dominant over the mind and taken it away.
Derivative: عَقَرُوا from عَقَرَ which means to slaughter and with a camel to cut one of its legs (i.e., hamstring it) so that it falls down and can be slaughtered.

Derivative: فَأْتِيَا is from أَتَى which means to come, arrive or go to somewhere without a problem i.e., is indicative of their being no obstacles in the way from you going to or reaching your destination.

- مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ can mean either from the people i.e., you choose to approach men for intimacy from the people (even though women are present)?! Or it can refer to them being the first from humankind to engage in such a practise in this manner as a nation.
Derivative: الْقَالِينَ – Those who detest something and want nothing to do with it.
Derivative: الغَابِرِينَ means those who were left behind and remained.

Derivative: الأَيْكَة refers to a forest or thicket of entwined trees i.e., a dense grouping of trees. The people of الأَيْكَة were those whom Shu’aib (as) was sent to and الأَيْكَة was close to Madyan.
Derivative: المُخْسِرِينَ means to cause or create a loss (i.e., for others). It carries the meaning of short-changing and giving less than what is rightfully due.

- A piece from or of the sky. It doesn’t mean the sky itself as we see it, but rather something from the sky that has been sent down or something from up high like a part of a mountain falling down, in the case where سَماء refers to something high that it looked up at.
Derivative: الكَافِرِينَ means those who are in denial or ungrateful here and not the meaning of being a disbeliever.
Derivative: الكِتاب refers to something that was physically written regardless of what it was written upon.
Derivative: الضَّالِينَ here means those who were lost or misguided and ignorant i.e., before the guidance of Allah came to him.
Derivative: سَلَكْنَا – We cause to enter or insert.
Derivative: بَغْتَةً encompasses the meanings of: Out of nowhere, without warning and all of the sudden.
Derivative: لَمَّا means “when” referring to the time of the occurrence of something. In this Verse: “I fled from you “when” I feared you (i.e., then at that time)”.
- “To lower your wing” to someone, means to be humble towards them, kind and tender.
Derivative: تَمُنُّ from مَنَّ which means to show or grant a favour.
Derivative: أَثِيمٍ – Someone who is always engaged in sin or in receipt of sin due to his acts.
Derivative: أَفَّاكٍ – A terrible and perpetual liar.
Derivative: الغَاوُونَ – Those who are lost and misled.
Derivative: يَهِيمُونَ is from هَامَ – يَهِيمُ and it means to wander aimlessly.
Derivative: انتَصَرُوا from اِنِتَصَرَ to come to the aid of, to be victorious and to take revenge.

Derivative: ما (what) is used for non-humans i.e., inanimate objects and animals whilst مَنْ (who) is used for humans and Allah. The use of مَا instead of مَنْ is to downplay, ridicule or show contempt for the object or subject matter it is attached to.

Derivative: مُوْقِنِينَ are those who wish to attain certainty or ascertain a matter without any doubts regarding a matter. It comes from إِيقَان which in turn comes from يَقِينٌ. Yaqeen includes ‘Ilm (knowledge), the removal of doubt and the realisation and verification of a matter.

Derivative: بَاخِعٌ from the verb بَخَعَ which means to grieve or rage to the point that you consume yourself, become overwhelmed and the matter causing your grief or rage kills you. Here, Allah is addressing His Prophet and saying: “Are you going to kill yourself in grief or worry so much that it kills you, that they are not going to become believers?”
Derivative: ثُعْبَانٌ is a large or huge snake.

Derivative: أَرْجِهْ from أَرْجَأ – يُرْجِي – إِرْجَاء – which means to delay for a later time or to keep around. In this context it can mean: “Let Musa (as) go but keep a watch on him” and it can also mean “Delay punishing him for now”.
Derivative: مِيقَات is from وَقْت (time) and means the meeting place or meeting time i.e., time and place of appointment.
Derivative: خَاضِعِينَ the plural of خَاضِعٌ which means to be humble, pliant and submissive.
Derivative: تَلْقَفُ from لَقِفَ means to seize, grab hold of or snatch something quickly. Here it means to snatch and swallow quickly.
Derivative: أُلْقِىَ means to be thrown down i.e., the sorcerers were immediately “thrown down” in prostration to Allah when they witnessed what Musa (as) brought.
Derivative: مُحْدَث means new; something that is fresh and new to the minds.
Derivative: شِرْذِمَةٌ A small or insignificant group or band of powerless people.
this can hold two possible meanings: either, that they (i.e., bani isra’eel) are enraged with us, or that they have made us enraged with them.
Derivative: مُدْرَكُونَ from إِدرَاك which means to be caught up with.
Derivative: فِرْقٍ means a distinct division or part.
Derivative: طَّوْدِ is a wide tall mountain.
Derivative: اِنْفَلَقَ - اِنْفِلاق means to break open or split apart. It is used when something cracks open into two equal halves.
Derivative: أَزْلَفْنَا from أَزْلَفَ – إِزْلاف means to bring really close but not quite in contact.

- إِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ – “Indeed, they are an enemy”. إِنَّهُمْ is in the plural form (هُم) whilst عَدُوٌّ (enemy) is singular. This can be understood to conform with the understanding that disbelief is one Millah i.e., it all falls under one category and is regarded as being one religion in the case that all the false idols that they worshipped are regarded as one and the same manifestation of disbelief and are all an enemy to Ibrahim (as). It can also mean that each and every single thing that you worship is an enemy to me.

Derivative: مَرِضْتُ is in the 1st person form whilst all other statements of Ibrahim (as) in his series of statements here in this Surah are in the third person form stating that Allah (He) created me, guided me, fed me, gave me drink, cured me, causes me to die and brings me back to life (again). مَرِضْتُ (I become sick) is used instead of “He caused me to become sick”. That can be understood from the angle that sickness is a flaw or defect and that Ibrahim (as) does not want to attribute that to Allah.

- This combination of العَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ is repeated over and over again (ten times) in Surah Ash-Shu’ara’ but is not found in any other Surah. العَزِيز means He is the One who has the ultimate authority and الرَّحِيم means He is always merciful. It can be understood to indicate that Allah has the ultimate power, might and authority to send His punishment upon the disbelievers and yet He continues to show His mercy.
Derivative: بُرِّزَت is from بَرَّزَ which means to be clearly shown and manifested in front of you.
Derivative: كُبْكِبُوا from كَبْكَبَ to be toppled upside down i.e., dropped or thrown face first into the fire of hell.
Derivative: يَخْتَصِمُونَ means to be engaged in vicious argument, conflict and debate.
Derivative: تَٱللَّهِ – “By Allah” is one of the forms of swearing by Allah like وَاللهِ and باللَّهِ and has been understood to be used for the purpose of expressing emphasis (تَوْكِيد).
